exemplar.title: Investigating the Prior Context Effect on Recall of an Ambiguous Text

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the Prior Context Effect on Recall of an Ambiguous Text

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and well-stated aim with explicit explanation of its educational relevance
Accurate and concise description of schema theory linked to the investigation
Hypotheses clearly identify and operationalize both IV and DV
weaknesses: Schema theory description is dense and could be more succinct
Minor typographical errors (e.g., âMatic primingâ) reduce precision
Brief gaps in describing Bransford and Johnsonâs methodology leave rationale incomplete
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction satisfies all top-band descriptors: the aim is stated and its relevance is explained, theory/model is described and connected, and variables are operationalized. However, minor presentation issues and opportunities to tighten explanations prevent a perfect 6, so a score of 5 appropriately reflects high quality with room for polish.

strengths: Independent-measures design is explained with rationale
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are described with some justification
Controlled variables and materials are listed and their selection briefly justified
weaknesses: Depth of explanation on design rationale could be increased (e.g., order effects)
Generalizability concerns (age range) are noted but not fully addressed
Material descriptions are overly detailed and could be grouped more concisely
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section reaches the middle band (3â4) by providing explanations rather than mere descriptions. Explanations of design choice, sampling, and controls show understanding, but limited depth and minor clarity issues place it at a solid 3 out of 4.

strengths: Appropriate use of both descriptive and inferential statistics
Graphical presentation addresses the hypothesis with correct format
Findings are linked to hypothesis in interpretation
weaknesses: Statistical justifications (e.g., one-tailed test) are superficial
Graph lacks full labelling and formal titles
Interpretation is correct but would benefit from deeper discussion of effect size and variance
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis demonstrates application of correct statistical techniques and integration with the hypothesis, consistent with band 3â4. Minor inaccuracies, insufficient labelling, and limited interpretive depth cap the score at 3 out of 6.

strengths: Findings discussion references schema theory and original study comparison
Strengths and limitations are relevant and linked to the investigation
Proposed modifications are clearly tied to limitations with brief justification
weaknesses: Discussion is accurate but lacks critical depth
Quantification of reliability improvements is not explored
Modifications rationale could better address validity trade-offs
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation meets the 3â4 band by relating findings to theory, stating relevant strengths/limitations, and linking modifications to limitations. The moderate depth and room for more critical justification justify a 3 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph with error bars is provided and clearly contrasts the two conditions, addressing the hypothesis.  Nonetheless, axis labels/units are very minimal and the title is informal; thus there are **minor presentation errors**.  This matches the 3â4 descriptor (score 3).

The student states the statistical findings, links them to the rejection of the null, and offers a brief interpretation (schema activation improved recall).  The interpretation is correct but **surface-level** and lacks detailed data commentary, so it fits the 3â4 band, score 3.

Both research and null hypotheses are presented.  The IV (receiving prior context through thematic priming / none) and DV (number of ideas recalled) are **identified and operationalised**âthe DV is quantified out of 18 ideas and the IV is defined by the wording added to the recording.  This matches the 5â6 descriptor.

Sampling technique (convenience) and participant characteristics (number, gender, L1 vs L2 English, profession) are given and some rationale is offered (availability, no parental consent, bias acknowledged).  This constitutes an **explanation** rather than mere description, so 3â4 band is appropriate; depth is moderate, so a 3 is awarded.

Schema theory is accurately outlined (origin with Bartlett, functions of schemas, priming) and **the connection to the current investigation is explained**: the study tests whether thematic priming (prior context) activates the relevant schema and thus influences recall.  The coverage is largely correct and clearly tied to the chosen variables, meeting the top-band requirement.

Findings are related back to schema theory, referencing how activated schemas aid encoding; comparison with Bransford & Johnson is included.  The discussion is accurate but relatively concise and not deeply critical, matching the 3â4 band (score 3).

The student states a clear aim (âTo investigate the prior context effect on recall of an ambiguous textâ) and **explicitly explains its relevance** by linking the outcome to educational practice and the teaching profession.  The explanation moves beyond a simple mention by discussing how schema activation might inform classroom instruction.  This satisfies the descriptor for the 5â6 band.

Several **relevant strengths and limitations are described** (sample restriction, independent measures variability, scoring subjectivity, ecological validity).  Explanations are present but not always developed in depth; hence it earns the 3â4 band, score 3.

Controlled variables are listed *and* the choice of some controls is justified (pre-recording to standardise tone, quiet room to minimise distraction).  Materials are listed with brief purpose notes.  That meets the requirement for âexplainedâ, albeit briefly, placing it in the 3â4 band (score 3).

Proposed modifications (narrower age range, stricter scoring rubric, individual testing) are **clearly linked to the stated limitations and justified** in brief.  While sound, the justification is not especially extensive, placing it comfortably in the 3â4 band, score 3.

The student goes beyond a basic description by **explaining why an independent-measures design was chosen (avoid order effects/demand characteristics) and how random allocation addresses participant variability**.  The explanation is present but not especially deep or critical, fitting the middle 3â4 band (score 3).

Both descriptive and inferential statistics are applied and appropriate; however, the analytical write-up relies heavily on online calculators, and the rationale for a *one-tailed* test is limited.  Potential issues (unequal group sizes, small n) are not discussed.  Minor inaccuracies and superficial justification keep this in the 3â4 band, score 3.

annotations.comment: The title clearly and concisely communicates the focus of the investigation, orienting the reader immediately to the studyâs purpose.
Candidate and session details are administrative and interrupt flow; consider moving these to a footnote or header to maintain narrative clarity.
The description of schema theory is accurate but dense; briefly summarise its core tenets and then explicitly link each to how schema activation will be measured.
âMatic primingâ appears to be a typo; replace with âthematic primingâ for precision and consistency with standard terminology.
The summary of Bransford and Johnsonâs methodology ends abruptly (âAll participants were thenâ¦â); complete this to ensure the rationale and procedure are clear.
The paragraph outlining recall scoring is concise and clearly describes operationalisation of the DV (18 key ideas). Well done.
Independent and dependent variables are listed but lack operational definitions here; specify exactly how âreceiving prior contextâ is administered and measured.
Ensure consistency in hypothesis notation: the null hypothesis should use matching bold or italic formatting (e.g., $$H_0$$) throughout.
The aim statement is clear and its educational relevance is well explained in the introduction; it aligns with pedagogical practice.
Consider operationalising expected effect size in the directional hypothesis (e.g., medium effect, Cohenâs $$d$$), to make statistical expectations explicit.
The rationale for using an independent-measures design is sound, but deepen the explanation by addressing potential order effects in more detail.
Sample description is comprehensive; however, clarify why age range was not selected and discuss how this may affect generalisability.
Some list items include stray variables (e.g., $$\overline{a}$$); remove OCR artifacts to improve readability and accuracy.
Material descriptions are exhaustive but overly detailed; consider grouping items by function and justifying key materials concisely.
The procedure section is detailed and logically ordered, ensuring reproducibility of each step.
Ethical considerations are addressed, but elaborate on how minimal deception was mitigated in the debrief to reduce post-test bias.
The outlier removal is noted, but include a fuller justification (e.g., impact on variance, test robustness) and consider sensitivity analysis.
Table 1 effectively displays central tendency and dispersion; consider adding confidence intervals to enhance inferential insight.
Graph 1 addresses the hypothesis but lacks clear axis labels (e.g., âMean ideas recalledâ vs. âConditionâ); revise for full self-containment.
Interpretation connects data to schema activation, but depth could be increased with discussion of practical effect size and variance context.
Rationale for the one-tailed test is briefly mentioned; expand on why a directional hypothesis specifically warrants this choice versus two-tailed.
The discussion accurately ties findings to schema theory and original study results; this reinforces theoretical linkage.
The strengths section is relevant but could be enhanced by quantifying reliability improvements (e.g., inter-rater reliability coefficient).
The limitation discussion is comprehensive and addresses both sampling and procedural issues, demonstrating critical insight.
Note that narrowing the sample improves internal validity but may increase bias; clarify this trade-off in your modification section.
Your proposed modifications link logically to stated limitations; further justify by explaining how each change would address a specific threat to validity.
The final note on schema theoryâs operationalisation challenge is valid; suggest including an alternative measure (e.g., reaction time) to strengthen causal claims.
Several references show inconsistent citation formatting (e.g., Bartlett entry); ensure uniform adherence to APA or IB style guidelines.
